---
title: "Synthetic Control"
subtitle: "Constructing a data-driven counterfactual for a single treated unit"
---

## Overview

What happens when only *one* unit is treated --- a single region launches a reform, a single municipality adopts a program --- and there is no natural comparison group? Difference-in-differences needs a credible control whose trend matches the treated unit's; with one treated region and twenty candidate controls, which do you pick?

The synthetic control method answers: don't pick one --- build one. It constructs a weighted average of untreated "donor" units that closely tracks the treated unit's *pre-treatment* trajectory, then uses that synthetic unit as the counterfactual after treatment. The gap between the treated unit and its synthetic twin is the estimated effect. Because the weights are chosen transparently and constrained to avoid extrapolation, the method has become a workhorse for comparative case studies.

This module covers how the synthetic unit is built, how to do inference when there is effectively one treated unit (placebo and permutation tests), the method's extensions and failure modes, and a full worked Swedish case study.

## Pages in This Module

- [The Synthetic Control Method](method.qmd) --- Building a counterfactual from a weighted donor pool.
- [Inference for Synthetic Control](inference.qmd) --- Placebo tests, RMSPE ratios, and permutation.
- [Extensions and Practical Issues](extensions.qmd) --- When it works, augmented SCM, and pitfalls.
- [Case Study: A Swedish Regional Reform](case-study.qmd) --- Synthetic control from start to finish.
